content.text:
Please analyze this codebase and create a CLAUDE.md file, which will be given to future instances of Claude Code to operate in this repository.

What to add:
1. Commands that will be commonly used, such as how to build, lint, and run tests. Include the necessary commands to develop in this codebase, such as how to run a single test.
2. High-level code architecture and structure so that future instances can be productive more quickly. Focus on the "big picture" architecture that requires reading multiple files to understand.

Usage notes:
- If there's already a CLAUDE.md, suggest improvements to it.
- When you make the initial CLAUDE.md, do not repeat yourself and do not include obvious instructions like "Provide helpful error messages to users", "Write unit tests for all new utilities", "Never include sensitive information (API keys, tokens) in code or commits".
- Avoid listing every component or file structure that can be easily discovered.
- Don't include generic development practices.
- If there are Cursor rules (in .cursor/rules/ or .cursorrules) or Copilot rules (in .github/copilot-instructions.md), make sure to include the important parts.
- If there is a README.md, make sure to include the important parts.
- Do not make up information such as "Common Development Tasks", "Tips for Development", "Support and Documentation" unless this is expressly included in other files that you read.
- Be sure to prefix the file with the following text:

```
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
```

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- web-design-guidelines: Review UI code for Web Interface Guidelines compliance. Use when asked to "review my UI", "check accessibility", "audit design", "review UX", or "check my site against best practices".
- react-best-practices: React and Next.js performance optimization guidelines from Vercel Engineering. This skill should be used when writing, reviewing, or refactoring React/Next.js code to ensure optimal performance patterns. Triggers on tasks involving React components, Next.js pages, data fetching, bundle optimization, or performance improvements.
- frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications (examples include websites, landing pages, dashboards, React components, HTML/CSS layouts, or when styling/beautifying any web UI). Generates creative, polished code and UI design that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- web-design-guidelines: Review UI code for Web Interface Guidelines compliance. Use when asked to "review my UI", "check accessibility", "audit design", "review UX", or "check my site against best practices".
- shadcn: Manages shadcn components and projects — adding, searching, fixing, debugging, styling, and composing UI. Provides project context, component docs, and usage examples. Applies when working with shadcn/ui, component registries, presets, --preset codes, or any project with a components.json file. Also triggers for "shadcn init", "create an app with --preset", or "switch to --preset".
- supabase-postgres-best-practices: Postgres performance optimization and best practices from Supabase. Use this skill when writing, reviewing, or optimizing Postgres queries, schema designs, or database configurations.
- supabase: Use when doing ANY task involving Supabase. Triggers: Supabase products (Database, Auth, Edge Functions, Realtime, Storage, Vectors, Cron, Queues); client libraries and SSR integrations (supabase-js, @supabase/ssr) in Next.js, React, SvelteKit, Astro, Remix; auth issues (login, logout, sessions, JWT, cookies, getSession, getUser, getClaims, RLS); Supabase CLI or MCP server; schema changes, migrations, security audits, Postgres extensions (pg_graphql, pg_cron, pg_vector).
- vercel-react-best-practices: React and Next.js performance optimization guidelines from Vercel Engineering. This skill should be used when writing, reviewing, or refactoring React/Next.js code to ensure optimal performance patterns. Triggers on tasks involving React components, Next.js pages, data fetching, bundle optimization, or performance improvements.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Big Hack Berlin — Qontext Track
2	
3	**Event:** Tech Europe Hack, Berlin
4	**Start:** 2026-04-25 · **Submission deadline:** Sunday 14:00
5	**Track:** [Qontext](https://qontext.ai/?utm_source=luma) — turn fragmented company data into a context base AI can operate on
6	**Repo:** https://github.com/lassejohannis/TechEurope
7	
8	---
9	
10	## 1. Challenge
11	
12	Most AI systems reconstruct company reality at runtime by pulling scattered facts from mail, CRM, policies, tickets, docs, and chat — hoping the prompt is good enough. Qontext argues this doesn't scale.
13	
14	**Input:** simulated enterprise dataset — email, CRM, HR, policy documents, collaboration/workspace data, IT service data, business records (provided on event day).
15	
16	**Output:** a virtual file system + graph that documents the business and is legible to both machines and humans.
17	
18	### Goal
19	
20	Build a system that turns the dataset into a structured company memory:
21	
22	- **Virtual file system** that documents the business:
23	  - static data (employees, customers, products)
24	  - procedural knowledge (processes, SOPs, rules)
25	  - trajectory information (tasks, projects, progress)
26	- **Explicit references** — links between files, and links to the underlying source records.
27	- **Interface(s)** for AI systems to retrieve context efficiently, and for business users + AI to inspect, validate, edit, and extend the company memory.
28	
29	### Criteria for a strong solution
30	
31	- Generalize beyond the provided dataset and data format.
32	- Resolve easy information conflicts automatically; involve humans where ambiguity actually matters.
33	- Preserve **provenance at the fact level** and update automatically when source facts change.
34	
35	### Anti-patterns
36	
37	- Dumping markdown into folders.
38	- Building a documentation chatbot.
39	
40	The brief emphasizes: explainable, editable, robust under change, useful in practice. Cover both **graph construction** and **retrieval**. Treat the VFS as a **product surface**, not just storage.
41	
42	Detail: [docs/qontext-case.md](docs/qontext-case.md)
43	
44	---
45	
46	## 2. Submission Requirements
47	
48	- **Deadline:** Sunday 14:00
49	- **Team:** max. 5 people
50	- **Partner tech:** min. **3 partner technologies** (Aikido excluded from the 3)
51	- **Newly built** at the hackathon (boilerplates allowed)
52	
53	### What to submit
54	
55	- **2-minute video demo** (Loom or equivalent) — live walkthrough of key features.
56	- **Public GitHub repo** — README with setup + install instructions, clear documentation of all APIs/frameworks/tools, enough technical docs for the jury.
57	
58	### Competition format — two stages
59	
60	1. **Pre-Selection** — 8 finalist teams (1 per track) advance. Judging: creativity, technical complexity, bonus for effective use of partner tech.
61	2. **Finalist Stage** — 5-min live pitch to jury + audience. Jury picks top 3 winners.
62	
63	Detail: [docs/hack-rules.md](docs/hack-rules.md)
64	
65	---
66	
67	## 3. Technology Partners
68	
69	Rule recap: min. 3 required. **Aikido is NOT eligible as one of the 3.**
70	
71	| Partner | What it is | Onboarding / docs |
72	|---|---|---|
73	| [Google DeepMind](https://deepmind.google/) | Frontier multimodal models (Gemini). Temporary accounts issued on site. | [How to use the temp account](https://goo.gle/hackathon-account) |
74	| [Tavily](https://www.tavily.com/) | Real-time search, extraction, research, web crawling API. | [docs.tavily.com](https://docs.tavily.com/welcome) — 1000 free credits on signup; top-up code in `NOTES.local.md` |
75	| [Lovable](https://lovable.dev) | AI-chat app/website builder. | Pro-Plan-1 discount code in `NOTES.local.md` — Settings → Plans & Credits → monthly plan → checkout |
76	| [Gradium](https://gradium.ai/) | Voice AI models for realtime interactions. | [api_docs](https://gradium.ai/api_docs.html) — after signup, ping Pratim on site or Discord `#gradium` with your org name |
77	| [Entire](https://entire.io/?utm_source=luma) | Developer platform for agent-human collaboration. | [CLI installation](https://docs.entire.io/cli/installation) |
78	| [Aikido](https://www.aikido.dev/) — ***not eligible as 1 of 3*** | Security scanning. Free Pro trial during the event. | [app.aikido.dev/login](https://app.aikido.dev/login) |
79	| [Pioneer](https://pioneer.ai/) by [Fastino](https://fastino.ai/) | "Models that train themselves." Synthetic data gen + eval + adaptive inference. | Onboarding page in the hack's Notion |
80	
81	Fit analysis + Path-A vs Path-B partner combo decision: [docs/stack.md](docs/stack.md).
82	
83	---
84	
85	## 4. Side Challenges
86	
87	Stackable on top of the Qontext track prize.
88	
89	| Challenge | Requirement | Prize |
90	|---|---|---|
91	| **Fastino — Best use of Pioneer** | Use Pioneer. Fine-tune a model that outperforms / replaces a general-purpose LLM API call. Thoughtful use of synthetic data, evals, adaptive inference. Bonus for creative GLiNER2. | **700€** (Mac Mini cash value) |
92	| **Aikido — Most Secure Build** | Create account, connect Git, connect repo, screenshot security report. | **1000€** |
93	| **Gradium — Best use of Gradium** | Use Gradium. Confirm in submission. | 900k Gradium credits + goodies |
94	| **Entire — Best use of Entire** | Use Entire. Confirm in submission. | $1000 Apple gift cards + Switch 2 + PS5 + Xbox |
95	
96	Detail: [docs/side-challenges.md](docs/side-challenges.md)
97	
98	---
99	
100	## 5. Product Direction
101	
102	We build **two layers + a demo surprise** — direct alignment with Qontext's stated direction (*"use-case agnostic context layer at the core + revenue intelligence use case on top"*).
103	
104	1. **Core Context Infrastructure** — horizontal, use-case-agnostic. Ingestion, entity resolution, graph, VFS, provenance, change streams. The thing every AI app should plug into.
105	2. **Revenue Intelligence App** — vertical proof-of-value sitting on top of the Core. Morning Action Feed with prepared drafts. Validates the platform with a real ROI story.
106	3. **Second App (live demo surprise)** — minimal HR or Finance view on the same Core, started live at pitch end. Proves horizontality.
107	
108	**Hard rule:** Core code must contain zero Revenue terminology. The Revenue App consumes only the Core's public Query API.
109	
110	Detail: [docs/team-briefing-technical.md](docs/team-briefing-technical.md) (engineering) and [docs/team-briefing-commercial.md](docs/team-briefing-commercial.md) (business + pitch).
111	
112	## 6. Stack (locked + open)
113	
114	### Locked
115	| Layer | Choice |
116	|---|---|
117	| Frontend | Vite + React + TypeScript + Tailwind + shadcn/ui |
118	| Graph viz | react-flow |
119	| Backend | FastAPI (Python 3.12) + uv |
120	| **Database (everything)** | **Supabase (Postgres + pgvector + Realtime + Auth + Storage)** |
121	| Graph store | Postgres `nodes` + `edges` tables, recursive CTEs |
122	| Vector store | pgvector (column on `entities` and `facts`) |
123	| LLM | **Gemini (Google DeepMind)** — partner-eligible |
124	| MCP server | `modelcontextprotocol` Python SDK |
125	| Connectors | Mock-from-JSON in `data/raw/` |
126	
127	### Open — partner combo (decide Day 1)
128	
129	Two viable paths, both stack with Aikido for the security side prize:
130	
131	**Option A — Technical heavyweight:** Gemini + Pioneer (fine-tuned GLiNER2) + Entire (HITL)
132	**Option B — Ecosystem play:** Gemini + Tavily (research) + Gradium (call STT)
133	
134	Detail + decision criteria: [docs/stack.md](docs/stack.md)
135	
136	**Total achievable prize stack:** Qontext track (1g gold/member + dinner) + Aikido 1000€ + side prizes from whichever partners we pick + Stage-2 finalist prizes.
137	
138	---
139	
140	## 7. Design docs
141	
142	**Read in order — most important first:**
143	
144	| # | Doc | What's in it |
145	|---|---|---|
146	| 1 | [docs/qontext-case.md](docs/qontext-case.md) | The sponsor brief verbatim |
147	| 2 | [docs/team-briefing-technical.md](docs/team-briefing-technical.md) | Engineering plan: two-layer architecture, Core Layer principles, demo flow (canonical) |
148	| 3 | [docs/team-briefing-commercial.md](docs/team-briefing-commercial.md) | Business + pitch: ICP, GTM, validation (Luca/Hypatos), pitch story |
149	| 4 | [docs/stack.md](docs/stack.md) | Stack decisions (locked) + open partner-combo question |
150	| 5 | [docs/data-model.md](docs/data-model.md) | `SourceRecord` / `Entity` / `Fact` / `Resolution` schemas |
151	| 6 | [docs/mcp-tools.md](docs/mcp-tools.md) | 5 agent-facing MCP tools with full I/O specs |
152	
153	**Background / operational:**
154	
155	| Doc | Purpose |
156	|---|---|
157	| [docs/team-brainstorm.md](docs/team-brainstorm.md) | Pain points, ICP, ideas A–E, market analysis — why we landed here |
158	| [docs/team-setup.md](docs/team-setup.md) | Pre-hack checklist, MCP auth steps, kickoff agenda, risk register |
159	| [docs/skills.md](docs/skills.md) | Claude Skills shipped with the repo and how to add more |
160	| [docs/hack-rules.md](docs/hack-rules.md) | Submission rules + competition format |
161	| [docs/side-challenges.md](docs/side-challenges.md) | Side prizes + stacking strategy |
162	
163	## 8. Prize overview
164	
165	- **Qontext track prize:** 1g real gold bar per team member + private dinner with Qontext
166	- **Side prizes:** see section 4
167	- **Stage-2 finalist prizes:** announced separately (top 3 after the live pitch)
168	
169	---
170	
171	## Project layout
172	
173	```
174	.
175	├── README.md                          ← this file (single source of truth)
176	├── NOTES.local.md                     ← gitignored; partner codes
177	├── .mcp.json                          ← project-scoped MCP servers (Supabase configured)
178	├── .claude/skills/                    ← Claude Skills auto-loaded for the team
179	│   ├── supabase/                        Supabase domain expertise + security checklist
180	│   ├── supabase-postgres-best-practices/  Indexing, RLS, locks, monitoring
181	│   ├── shadcn/                          Component composition, styling, forms, icons
182	│   ├── vercel-react-best-practices/     40+ React/Next.js performance rules
183	│   └── web-design-guidelines/           100+ UI / a11y / UX review rules
184	├── .agents/skills/                    ← same skills, mirrored for non-Claude tools
185	├── docs/
186	│   ├── qontext-case.md                  case brief (sponsor)
187	│   ├── hack-rules.md                    submission rules
188	│   ├── side-challenges.md               side prizes + stacking strategy
189	│   ├── team-briefing-technical.md       two-layer architecture, demo flow (canonical)
190	│   ├── team-briefing-commercial.md      ICP, GTM, validation, pitch story
191	│   ├── team-brainstorm.md               pain points, ideas A–E, market analysis
192	│   ├── stack.md                         stack decisions (locked + open)
193	│   ├── data-model.md                    shared schemas
194	│   ├── mcp-tools.md                     agent-facing API spec
195	│   ├── team-setup.md                    pre-hack checklist + MCP auth + kickoff
196	│   └── skills.md                        skill library (what's installed, how to add)
197	└── .gitignore
198	```
199	
200	**Status:** boilerplate is in place (Vite + React + TS + Tailwind + shadcn/ui + TanStack Query frontend; FastAPI + uv backend; Vite-proxied dev). Day-1 work fills in the Core Context Engine + Revenue Intelligence App on top of this scaffold.
201	
202	---
203	
204	## Quickstart
205	
206	Prerequisites: Node 20+, Python 3.12+, `uv`, `npm`.
207	
208	```bash
209	# Clone
210	git clone https://github.com/lassejohannis/TechEurope.git
211	cd TechEurope
212	
213	# Install everything
214	make install
215	
216	# Set env vars (copy templates, fill in values)
217	cp web/.env.example web/.env
218	cp server/.env.example server/.env
219	
220	# Run web (5173) + server (8000) together
221	make dev
222	```
223	
224	Open http://localhost:5173 — you should see "Boilerplate live." and a **Re-ping** button hitting `/api/hello` through the Vite proxy.
225	
226	Run them separately if you prefer:
227	```bash
228	make web      # Vite dev server, port 5173
229	make server   # FastAPI dev server, port 8000
230	make build    # Production build of the frontend
231	```
232	
233	### Project layout (top level)
234	
235	```
236	.
237	├── web/        Vite + React + TS frontend (Tailwind v4 + shadcn/ui + TanStack Query + react-flow + zustand)
238	├── server/    FastAPI backend (uv-managed; supabase + google-genai + mcp on the deps list)
239	├── Makefile   `make install / dev / web / server / build / clean`
240	├── docs/      Design docs (read order in section 7)
241	├── .mcp.json  Project-scoped MCP servers (Supabase + shadcn)
242	└── .claude/skills/  Project-scoped Claude Skills (5 installed)
243	```

| [Gradium](https://gradium.ai/) | Voice AI models for realtime interactions. | [api_docs](https://gradium.ai/api_docs.html) — after signup, ping Pratim on site or Discord `#gradium` with your org name |
| [Entire](https://entire.io/?utm_source=luma) | Developer platform for agent-human collaboration. | [CLI installation](https://docs.entire.io/cli/installation) |
| [Aikido](https://www.aikido.dev/) — ***not eligible as 1 of 3*** | Security scanning. Free Pro trial during the event. | [app.aikido.dev/login](https://app.aikido.dev/login) |
| [Pioneer](https://pioneer.ai/) by [Fastino](https://fastino.ai/) | "Models that train themselves." Synthetic data gen + eval + adaptive inference. | Onboarding page in the hack's Notion |

Fit analysis + Path-A vs Path-B partner combo decision: [docs/stack.md](docs/stack.md).

---

## 4. Side Challenges

Stackable on top of the Qontext track prize.

| Challenge | Requirement | Prize |
|---|---|---|
| **Fastino — Best use of Pioneer** | Use Pioneer. Fine-tune a model that outperforms / replaces a general-purpose LLM API call. Thoughtful use of synthetic data, evals, adaptive inference. Bonus for creative GLiNER2. | **700€** (Mac Mini cash value) |
| **Aikido — Most Secure Build** | Create account, connect Git, connect repo, screenshot security report. | **1000€** |
| **Gradium — Best use of Gradium** | Use Gradium. Confirm in submission. | 900k Gradium credits + goodies |
| **Entire — Best use of Entire** | Use Entire. Confirm in submission. | $1000 Apple gift cards + Switch 2 + PS5 + Xbox |

Detail: [docs/side-challenges.md](docs/side-challenges.md)

---

## 5. Product Direction

We build **two layers + a demo surprise** — direct alignment with Qontext's stated direction (*"use-case agnostic context layer at the core + revenue intelligence use case on top"*).

1. **Core Context Infrastructure** — horizontal, use-case-agnostic. Ingestion, entity resolution, graph, VFS, provenance, change streams. The thing every AI app should plug into.
2. **Revenue Intelligence App** — vertical proof-of-value sitting on top of the Core. Morning Action Feed with prepared drafts. Validates the platform with a real ROI story.
3. **Second App (live demo surprise)** — minimal HR or Finance view on the same Core, started live at pitch end. Proves horizontality.

**Hard rule:** Core code must contain zero Revenue terminology. The Revenue App consumes only the Core's public Query API.

Detail: [docs/team-briefing-technical.md](docs/team-briefing-technical.md) (engineering) and [docs/team-briefing-commercial.md](docs/team-briefing-commercial.md) (business + pitch).

## 6. Stack (locked + open)

### Locked
| Layer | Choice |
|---|---|
| Frontend | Vite + React + TypeScript + Tailwind + shadcn/ui |
| Graph viz | react-flow |
| Backend | FastAPI (Python 3.12) + uv |
| **Database (everything)** | **Supabase (Postgres + pgvector + Realtime + Auth + Storage)** |
| Graph store | Postgres `nodes` + `edges` tables, recursive CTEs |
| Vector store | pgvector (column on `entities` and `facts`) |
| LLM | **Gemini (Google DeepMind)** — partner-eligible |
| MCP server | `modelcontextprotocol` Python SDK |
| Connectors | Mock-from-JSON in `data/raw/` |

### Open — partner combo (decide Day 1)

Two viable paths, both stack with Aikido for the security side prize:

**Option A — Technical heavyweight:** Gemini + Pioneer (fine-tuned GLiNER2) + Entire (HITL)
**Option B — Ecosystem play:** Gemini + Tavily (research) + Gradium (call STT)

Detail + decision criteria: [docs/stack.md](docs/stack.md)

**Total achievable prize stack:** Qontext track (1g gold/member + dinner) + Aikido 1000€ + side prizes from whichever partners we pick + Stage-2 finalist prizes.

---

## 7. Design docs

**Read in order — most important first:**

| # | Doc | What's in it |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | [docs/qontext-case.md](docs/qontext-case.md) | The sponsor brief verbatim |
| 2 | [docs/team-briefing-technical.md](docs/team-briefing-technical.md) | Engineering plan: two-layer architecture, Core Layer principles, demo flow (canonical) |
| 3 | [docs/team-briefing-commercial.md](docs/team-briefing-commercial.md) | Business + pitch: ICP, GTM, validation (Luca/Hypatos), pitch story |
| 4 | [docs/stack.md](docs/stack.md) | Stack decisions (locked) + open partner-combo question |
| 5 | [docs/data-model.md](docs/data-model.md) | `SourceRecord` / `Entity` / `Fact` / `Resolution` schemas |
| 6 | [docs/mcp-tools.md](docs/mcp-tools.md) | 5 agent-facing MCP tools with full I/O specs |

**Background / operational:**

| Doc | Purpose |
|---|---|
| [docs/team-brainstorm.md](docs/team-brainstorm.md) | Pain points, ICP, ideas A–E, market analysis — why we landed here |
| [docs/team-setup.md](docs/team-setup.md) | Pre-hack checklist, MCP auth steps, kickoff agenda, risk register |
| [docs/skills.md](docs/skills.md) | Claude Skills shipped with the repo and how to add more |
| [docs/hack-rules.md](docs/hack-rules.md) | Submission rules + competition format |
| [docs/side-challenges.md](docs/side-challenges.md) | Side prizes + stacking strategy |

## 8. Prize overview

- **Qontext track prize:** 1g real gold bar per team member + private dinner with Qontext
- **Side prizes:** see section 4
- **Stage-2 finalist prizes:** announced separately (top 3 after the live pitch)

---

## Project layout

```
.
├── README.md                          ← this file (single source of truth)
├── NOTES.local.md                     ← gitignored; partner codes
├── .mcp.json                          ← project-scoped MCP servers (Supabase configured)
├── .claude/skills/                    ← Claude Skills auto-loaded for the team
│   ├── supabase/                        Supabase domain expertise + security checklist
│   ├── supabase-postgres-best-practices/  Indexing, RLS, locks, monitoring
│   ├── shadcn/                          Component composition, styling, forms, icons
│   ├── vercel-react-best-practices/     40+ React/Next.js performance rules
│   └── web-design-guidelines/           100+ UI / a11y / UX review rules
├── .agents/skills/                    ← same skills, mirrored for non-Claude tools
├── docs/
│   ├── qontext-case.md                  case brief (sponsor)
│   ├── hack-rules.md                    submission rules
│   ├── side-challenges.md               side prizes + stacking strategy
│   ├── team-briefing-technical.md       two-layer architecture, demo flow (canonical)
│   ├── team-briefing-commercial.md      ICP, GTM, validation, pitch story
│   ├── team-brainstorm.md               pain points, ideas A–E, market analysis

```

**Status:** boilerplate is in place (Vite + React + TS + Tailwind + shadcn/ui + TanStack Query frontend; FastAPI + uv backend; Vite-proxied dev). Day-1 work fills in the Core Context Engine + Revenue Intelligence App on top of this scaffold.

---

## Quickstart

Prerequisites: Node 20+, Python 3.12+, `uv`, `npm`.

```bash
# Clone
git clone https://github.com/lassejohannis/TechEurope.git
cd TechEurope

# Install everything
make install

# Set env vars (copy templates, fill in values)
cp web/.env.example web/.env
cp server/.env.example server/.env

# Run web (5173) + server (8000) together
make dev
```

Open http://localhost:5173 — you should see "Boilerplate live." and a **Re-ping** button hitting `/api/hello` through the Vite proxy.

Run them separately if you prefer:
```bash
make web      # Vite dev server, port 5173
make server   # FastAPI dev server, port 8000
make build    # Production build of the frontend
```

### Project layout (top level)

```
.
├── web/        Vite + React + TS frontend (Tailwind v4 + shadcn/ui + TanStack Query + react-flow + zustand)
├── server/    FastAPI backend (uv-managed; supabase + google-genai + mcp on the deps list)
├── Makefile   `make install / dev / web / server / build / clean`
├── docs/      Design docs (read order in section 7)
├── .mcp.json  Project-scoped MCP servers (Supabase + shadcn)
└── .claude/skills/  Project-scoped Claude Skills (5 installed)
```

"""CLI entry: `uv run server` starts the FastAPI dev server."""

from __future__ import annotations

import uvicorn

from server.config import settings


def main() -> None:
    uvicorn.run(
        "server.main:app",
        host=settings.api_host,
        port=settings.api_port,
        reload=True,
    )


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
"""FastAPI app — single process exposing the Query API for both layers."""

from __future__ import annotations

from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware
from pydantic import BaseModel

from server.config import settings

app = FastAPI(
    title="Tech Europe — Context Engine",
    version="0.1.0",
    description=(
        "Boilerplate for the Big Berlin Hack 2026 (Qontext track). "
        "Day-1 work fills in Core Context Engine endpoints + Revenue App routes."
    ),
)

app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=settings.api_cors_origins,
    allow_credentials=True,
    allow_methods=["*"],
    allow_headers=["*"],
)


class HealthResponse(BaseModel):
    status: str
    service: str
    version: str


@app.get("/api/health", response_model=HealthResponse)
async def health() -> HealthResponse:
    return HealthResponse(
        status="ok",
        service="tech-europe-server",
        version="0.1.0",
    )


@app.get("/api/hello")
async def hello() -> dict[str, str]:
    """Sanity-check endpoint the frontend pings on first load."""
    return {"message": "Hello from FastAPI — backend is reachable."}
"""Runtime config loaded from .env / environment variables."""

from __future__ import annotations

from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings, SettingsConfigDict


class Settings(BaseSettings):
    model_config = SettingsConfigDict(
        env_file=".env",
        env_file_encoding="utf-8",
        extra="ignore",
    )

    # FastAPI
    api_host: str = "127.0.0.1"
    api_port: int = 8000
    api_cors_origins: list[str] = ["http://localhost:5173"]

    # Supabase
    supabase_url: str = ""
    supabase_anon_key: str = ""
    supabase_service_key: str = ""

    # Gemini (Google DeepMind partner)
    gemini_api_key: str = ""
    gemini_model: str = "gemini-2.0-flash-exp"


settings = Settings()

1	# Technical Product Briefing (Team)
2	
3	Verbatim from team's Notion. Preserved for Day-1 kickoff reference.
4	
5	## Context
6	
7	Wir bauen für den Big Berlin Hack ein zweischichtiges Produkt im Qontext-Track. Die Challenge: Company-Reality wird heute bei jedem AI-Call neu rekonstruiert. Unsere Antwort: Ein horizontaler Context-Layer im Core plus Revenue-Intelligence-App als Proof-of-Value.
8	
9	Qontext's Direktive an uns: *„Use-case agnostic context layer at the core + revenue intelligence use case on top to visualize value."* Das ist nicht verhandelbar. Wer Revenue-Code im Core-Layer schreibt, hat die Mission verfehlt.
10	
11	---
12	
13	## Architektur-Prinzip
14	
15	Zwei Schichten, hart getrennt. Der Core-Layer weiß nicht was Sales ist. Die Revenue-App konsumiert nur die öffentliche Query-API des Core-Layers.
16	
17	Lackmus-Test: Wenn wir morgen eine Customer-Success-App, HR-App oder Finance-App oben drauf bauen würden, darf am Core-Layer kein Code geändert werden müssen.
18	
19	---
20	
21	## Layer 1: Core Context Engine
22	
23	### Virtual File System
24	
25	Abstrakte Schicht die heterogene Datenquellen als einheitlich adressierbare Nodes exponiert. Pfad-Struktur, nicht tabellarische Datenbank.
26	
27	Beispiel-Pfade:
28	
29	```
30	/companies/acme-corp/
31	/companies/acme-corp/contacts/max-mustermann
32	/companies/acme-corp/deals/q3-expansion
33	/companies/acme-corp/deals/q3-expansion/communications/
34	/companies/acme-corp/deals/q3-expansion/communications/2026-04-18-email-thread
35	```
36	
37	Jeder Node hat: Path, Type, Metadata, Content, Source, Timestamp, Attribution.
38	
39	Consumer-Apps fragen nicht Salesforce oder Gmail direkt. Sie fragen den VFS: „Gib mir alle Nodes unter /companies/acme-corp/deals/ mit Status=active."
40	
41	### Ingestion-Connectors
42	
43	**Minimum für MVP: 2 Quellen + Multi-Format-Reader.**
44	
45	- **Email-Mock (Gmail-Shape):** Threads mit Teilnehmern, Timestamps, Content — aus JSON
46	- **CRM-Mock (HubSpot-Shape):** Accounts, Contacts, Opportunities, Activities — aus JSON
47	
48	**Format-Reader (innerhalb der Adapter):** JSON, JSONL, CSV, **PDF** (Text via `pdfplumber`; strukturierte Felder bei Invoices/Resumes via Gemini + `instructor`-Schema). Doclinq als zusätzlicher Adapter falls Zeit bleibt — gleicher Pattern.
49	
50	**Plug-in-Architektur:** Connector-Interface ist dokumentiert (`docs/connector-template.md`), neuer Connector in <30 Min schreibbar — Skeleton + Beispiel im Repo.
51	
52	**Idempotent:** `content_hash` + deterministische IDs (`{source_type}:sha256:{hash}`). Mehrfach-Ingest desselben Records = Update, kein Duplikat.
53	
54	**Gestrichen für 48h:** Slack (OAuth-Hölle), Google Docs/Notion Live-Auth. Mock-Versionen sind die Demo.
55	
56	Jeder Connector ist stateless und minimal. Er pulled Rohdaten, normalisiert auf ein einheitliches Event-Schema, schiebt in den Layer. Keine Business-Logik im Connector.
57	
58	Events haben standardisiertes Format:
59	
60	```json
61	{
62	  "event_id": "...",
63	  "source": "...",
64	  "source_id": "...",
65	  "event_type": "...",
66	  "raw_content": "...",
67	  "extracted_entities": [],
68	  "timestamp": "...",
69	  "metadata": {}
70	}
71	```
72	
73	### Entity Resolution
74	
75	Erkennt dass dieselbe Person/Company in verschiedenen Quellen mit verschiedenen Identifikatoren auftaucht.
76	
77	**Tooling-Entscheidung (post-Cowork-Research, 2026-04-25):** **Hand-rolled deterministic-first cascade.** ~150-200 LoC Python + SQL. Kein Splink, kein Zingg, kein Cognee.
78	
79	Begründung: Probabilistische ER-Libraries optimieren auf große Korpora ODER labeled training pairs. Wir haben weder. 80% unserer Matches resolven deterministisch (`email == email`). Splink-Setup: 6-10h. Hand-rolled: 4h.
80	
81	**Stack:**
82	
83	- **Hand-rolled Cascade-Resolver** (200 LoC max) — siehe Pipeline unten
84	- **Gemini `text-embedding-004`** für Embeddings (Matryoshka auf 768d) — schon im Stack, kein Self-Host-Tax
85	- **Pre-Normalize vor Embed:** lowercase, Suffix-Strip ("Inc/Ltd/GmbH/BV"), Whitespace-Collapse — gibt mehr Lift als ein besseres Modell
86	- **pgvector HNSW** mit `m=16, ef_construction=64`, `hnsw.ef_search=100` (ingest) / `40` (query)
87	- **rapidfuzz** für String-Distance-Fallbacks
88	- **Pioneer (Fastino) GLiNER2-finetune** für E+R-Extraction am Hot-Path (Tier 3.5, siehe [`partner-tech.md`](partner-tech.md))
89	- **Gemini Flash** als Resolver-of-last-resort für Ambiguity-Inbox-Pairs (~$0.0001/Pair). Nie als Hot-Path-Matcher.
90	
91	**Cascade-Pipeline:**
92	
93	```python
94	def resolve(record: SourceRecord) -> Resolution:
95	    # Tier 1: hard IDs (idempotent, deterministic, ~80% der Matches)
96	    if hit := match_hard_id(record):  # email, domain, salesforce_id, slack_uid
97	        return Resolution(entity_id=hit, confidence=1.0, tier="hard_id")
98	
99	    # Tier 2: structured aliases (resolver-table lookup)
100	    if hit := match_alias(record):
101	        return Resolution(entity_id=hit, confidence=0.95, tier="alias")
102	
103	    # Tier 3: embedding kNN over canonical_name + key attrs
104	    candidates = pgvector_knn(record.embedding, k=10, threshold=0.82)
105	    if len(candidates) == 1 and candidates[0].score > 0.92:
106	        return Resolution(entity_id=candidates[0].id, confidence=candidates[0].score, tier="embedding")
107	
108	    # Tier 3.5: Pioneer-finetuned E+R extractor confirms relationship
109	    if hit := pioneer_extract_and_match(record, candidates):
110	        return Resolution(entity_id=hit, confidence=0.90, tier="pioneer")
111	
112	    # Tier 4: context heuristics (email domain → company etc.)
113	    if hit := match_context(record, candidates):
114	        return Resolution(entity_id=hit, confidence=0.85, tier="context")
115	
116	    # Tier 5: ambiguity inbox (Gemini Flash für Borderline-Cases optional)
117	    return Resolution(entity_id=None, candidates=candidates, tier="inbox")
118	```
119	
120	**Drei Resolver wired by entity type:** ein `resolve()`-Entry, Dispatch nach `entity_type`. Person-Resolver kennt Phonetic-Encodings, Company-Resolver kennt Suffix-Stripping, Product-Resolver ist deterministisch via SKU/GTIN. Cascade-Struktur identisch.
121	
122	**Schwellwerte:** auto-merge >0.92, ambiguity-inbox 0.82–0.92, reject <0.82. Audit-Log auf jede Auto-Merge-Decision.
123	
124	Bei Unsicherheit: Candidate-Set vorhalten. Nicht false-positive resolven. Merge ist günstig, Un-Merge ist Hölle.
125	
126	### Embedding-Strategie (Two-Tier)
127	
128	Wir embedden **nicht alles gleich**. Zwei Tiers:
129	
130	**Tier A — Name-Embedding (Default, alle Entities):**
131	```python
132	text = normalize(canonical_name + " " + key_attrs)
133	# normalize: lowercase, strip "Inc/Ltd/GmbH/BV", collapse whitespace
134	embedding = gemini_embed_004(text, dim=768)  # Matryoshka
135	```
136	Billig, schnell, gut genug für Resolution-Blocking.
137	
138	**Tier B — Inference-Embedding (opt-in, hot entities):**
139	Für "wichtige" Entities (Companies + Persons mit ≥5 verbundenen Facts) generieren wir zusätzlich ein **kontext-reiches Embedding**:
140	
141	```python
142	def inference_embedding(entity: Entity) -> Vector:
143	    context = collect_graph_neighbors(entity, depth=1)  
144	    # → role, department, managed_projects, recent_activity, ...
145	    summary = gemini_flash.summarize(entity, context)
146	    # → "Max Mustermann is Head of IT, manages Project Alpha + Beta, 
147	    #    has 2 open tickets, reports to Anna Schmidt."
148	    return gemini_embed_004(summary, dim=768)
149	```
150	
151	Storage: zweite Spalte `inference_embedding VECTOR(768)` auf `entities`. Separater HNSW-Index.
152	
153	**Warum Two-Tier:**
154	- Tier A trägt 95% der Resolution-Workload (billig)
155	- Tier B macht **Semantic Search dramatisch besser** — *"Senior Engineers with high ticket load"* findet auch Personen, deren Titel "Senior" gar nicht enthält
156	- Tier B ist re-computed nur via `needs_refresh`-Flag, nicht eager bei jedem Edge-Update
157	
158	**Demo-Wert:** Cross-Domain Discovery — eine Query findet semantisch ähnliche Konzepte über verschiedene Datenquellen hinweg. Das ist sichtbar besser als naive Name-Embeddings.
159	
160	### Hybrid Search (search_memory MCP-Tool)
161	
162	`search_memory` ist explicit **3-Stage Hybrid**: Semantic ∩ Structural → Rerank.
163	
164	```python
165	def search_memory(query: str, k: int = 10) -> list[Result]:
166	    # Stage 1: Semantic
167	    q_emb = gemini_embed_004(query, dim=768)
168	    semantic = pgvector_knn(
169	        q_emb, 
170	        col="inference_embedding",  # Tier-B falls vorhanden, fallback Tier-A
171	        k=20, 
172	        threshold=0.7
173	    )
174	
175	    # Stage 2: Structural — Entity-Mentions aus Query extrahieren, 1-Hop traversieren
176	    mentions = pioneer_extract_mentions(query)  # Pioneer-finetuned, 2. Use!
177	    structural = []
178	    for m in mentions:
179	        entity = resolve_mention(m)
180	        if entity:
181	            structural += graph_traverse(entity, depth=1)
182	
183	    # Stage 3: Combine + Rerank
184	    candidates = intersect_or_union(semantic, structural)  # intersect bei viel signal, union bei wenig
185	    return rerank_by_confidence_and_recency(candidates)[:k]
186	```
187	
188	**Warum Hybrid:**
189	- Pure Semantic: findet ähnliche Konzepte aber ignoriert Struktur ("alle Senior Engineers" → ja, aber wie viele Tickets haben sie?)
190	- Pure Graph: präzise Pfade aber keine Fuzzy-Begriffe ("MANAGES → IT_DEPT" findet nichts wenn Query "leitet die IT" sagt)
191	- Hybrid: **Best of both.** Intersection erhöht Precision, Union erhöht Recall.
192	
193	**Pioneer-Bonus:** Tier 3.5 wird **zweitgenutzt** für Entity-Mention-Extraction aus der Query. Stärkt den Side-Prize-Claim ("our fine-tuned model is the backbone of both ingestion AND query").
194	
195	### Graph Construction
196	
197	Entitäten sind Nodes. Beziehungen zwischen ihnen sind typisierte Edges.
198	
199	**Node-Types:** `Person`, `Company`, `Product`, `Document`, `Communication` + use-case-spezifische via Config (`Deal`, `Project`, `Ticket`...). Document und Communication sind first-class Entities mit Edges zu Participants/Authors, nicht nur SourceRecords.
200	
201	**Edge-Types (vollständig):** `works_at`, `manages`, `authored`, `references`, `mentions`, `owns`, `assigned_to`, `participant_in`, `related_to`. Plus use-case-spezifische via Config (`champion_of` etc.).
202	
203	**Konfigurierbar, nicht hardcoded:** Entity- und Edge-Types werden als **YAML-Ontologien** in `config/ontologies/*.yaml` definiert (dev-friendly, version-controlled, PR-diffbar) und beim Boot in `entity_type_config` / `edge_type_config` Postgres-Tabellen geladen (runtime-changeable, single source of truth). Ingestion validiert dagegen — neuer Use Case = neue YAML-Datei + Reload, kein Code-Change.
204	
205	```yaml
206	# config/ontologies/hr.yaml
207	entities:
208	  Employee:
209	    properties:
210	      - {name: id, type: string}
211	      - {name: name, type: string}
212	      - {name: email, type: string}
213	  Department:
214	    properties:
215	      - {name: id, type: string}
216	      - {name: name, type: string}
217	relationships:
218	  works_in:
219	    from: Employee
220	    to: Department
221	    properties:
222	      - {name: since, type: date}
223	```
224	
225	Boot-Loader: `config/ontologies/*.yaml → ontology_loader.py → INSERT INTO {entity,edge}_type_config`. Onthologie-Hot-Reload-Endpoint für Demo (`POST /admin/reload-ontologies`).
226	
227	Edges tragen Metadata: `source_record_id`, `confidence`, `valid_from`, `valid_to`. Bi-direktional traversierbar (Indexes auf `(subject_id, predicate)` UND `(object_id, predicate)`).
228	
229	Der Graph wird on-ingestion gebaut und inkrementell aktualisiert. Nicht alles ist ein Graph-Query, aber alles was Beziehungen braucht geht über den Graph.
230	
231	### Temporal State
232	
233	Jeder Fakt hat Validity-Span. Nicht nur „Max ist Champion von Deal X" sondern „Max war Champion von 2026-02-01 bis 2026-04-10, danach verifiziert keine Aktivität mehr."
234	
235	Queries müssen zeitlich parametrisierbar sein: „Was war wahr am Datum Y?" muss funktionieren, nicht nur „Was ist wahr jetzt?"
236	
237	**Implementierung (Postgres-konkret, post-Cowork):** `tstzrange` + `EXCLUDE USING GIST` + Supersede-Trigger. Keine `temporal_tables`-Extension (Supabase ships sie nicht).
238	
239	```sql
240	CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS btree_gist;
241	
242	CREATE TABLE facts (
243	  id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
244	  subject_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES entities(id),
245	  predicate TEXT NOT NULL,                  -- string, nicht FK; flexibler
246	  object_id UUID REFERENCES entities(id),   -- nullable: entity-zu-entity
247	  object_literal JSONB,                     -- nullable: skalare Werte
248	  confidence NUMERIC(3,2) NOT NULL,
249	  source_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES source_records(id),
250	  derivation TEXT NOT NULL,                 -- "llm:gemini-2.0-flash", "rule:domain_match", "pioneer:gliner2-v1"
251	  valid_from TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
252	  valid_to TIMESTAMPTZ,                     -- NULL = open-ended (current)
253	  recorded_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
254	  superseded_by UUID REFERENCES facts(id),
255	  source_hash TEXT,                         -- für lazy re-derivation
256	  needs_refresh BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE
257	);
258	
259	ALTER TABLE facts
260	  ADD CONSTRAINT facts_no_overlap
261	  EXCLUDE USING GIST (
262	    subject_id WITH =,
263	    predicate WITH =,
264	    tstzrange(valid_from, COALESCE(valid_to, 'infinity'::timestamptz)) WITH &&
265	  );
266	
267	CREATE INDEX facts_subject ON facts (subject_id, predicate);
268	CREATE INDEX facts_object ON facts (object_id);
269	CREATE INDEX facts_current ON facts (subject_id, predicate) WHERE valid_to IS NULL;
270	
271	-- Supersede trigger: insert auf gleichem (subject, predicate) schließt alten Record
272	CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION supersede_previous_fact()
273	RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
274	BEGIN
275	  UPDATE facts
276	     SET valid_to = NEW.valid_from,
277	         superseded_by = NEW.id
278	   WHERE subject_id = NEW.subject_id
279	     AND predicate = NEW.predicate
280	     AND valid_to IS NULL
281	     AND id <> NEW.id;
282	  RETURN NEW;
283	END;
284	$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;
285	
286	CREATE TRIGGER facts_supersede
287	  BEFORE INSERT ON facts
288	  FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION supersede_previous_fact();
289	```
290	
291	**Lazy Re-Derivation (statt eager Triggers):**
292	
293	```sql
294	-- on ingest pipeline:
295	UPDATE facts SET needs_refresh = TRUE
296	 WHERE source_id = $1 AND source_hash <> $2;
297	
298	-- on read or background batch:
299	SELECT id FROM facts WHERE needs_refresh = TRUE LIMIT 100;
300	```
301	
302	**Hash:** `hash(source_content_hash + extractor_version)` — sonst triggert ein Prompt-Change keine Re-Derivation.
303	
304	**Foot-guns:**
305	- Immer `tstzrange`, niemals `tsrange` (Mixed-Timezone-Bugs sind silent killers)
306	- "As-of"-Query nutzt GIST-indexable `@>` Containment
307	- Postgres 18 hat `WITHOUT OVERLAPS` nativ — Supabase ist auf PG15/16, also EXCLUDE-Pattern bleiben
308	
309	**Demo-Scope:** **Time-Travel-Slider in der Entity-Detail-View** (siehe Demo-Killer-Features). UI-Slider pin't `recorded_at` und `tstzrange @>`, Entity-Card-State updated live. *Single feature outweighs three of your other planned demos.*
310	
311	### Source Attribution
312	
313	Jeder Fakt im Layer trägt Herkunft. Downstream-Consumer kann auf Attribution zugreifen.
314	
315	Beispiel-Response auf Query „Ist Max Champion von Deal X?":
316	
317	```json
318	{
319	  "answer": true,
320	  "confidence": 0.87,
321	  "evidence": [
322	    {
323	      "source": "gong_transcript_2026-03-15",
324	      "timecode": "00:23:45",
325	      "quote": "Max said he would drive the approval process"
326	    },
327	    {
328	      "source": "salesforce_opportunity_456",
329	      "field": "primary_contact",
330	      "value": "Max Mustermann"
331	    }
332	  ]
333	}
334	```
335	
336	### Query API
337	
338	Einheitliche Schnittstelle für alle Downstream-Apps. Zwei Access-Patterns:
339	
340	**Path-based (VFS-Style):**
341	
342	```
343	GET /nodes/companies/acme-corp/deals/q3-expansion
344	GET /nodes/companies/acme-corp/deals?filter=active
345	```
346	
347	**Graph-based (Traversal-Style):**
348	
349	```
350	QUERY: Find all Persons where champion_of relates to Deal
351	       with status=active and last_activity > 14_days_ago
352	```
353	
354	Beide Patterns returnen strukturierte JSON mit Attribution.
355	
356	### Neo4j Read-only Projection (post 2026-04-25)
357	
358	Postgres bleibt Source-of-Truth. Neo4j ist eine **read-only Projection**, gepflegt via Supabase-Realtime.
359	
360	**Architektur:**
361	```
362	[Postgres facts/entities INSERT]
363	    │
364	    ▼ Supabase Realtime
365	[server/sync/neo4j_projection.py]
366	    │
367	    ▼ idempotent MERGE-Cypher
368	[Neo4j Aura Free-Tier]
369	    │
370	    ▼ Cypher
371	[FastAPI /query/cypher endpoint]
372	```
373	
374	**Routing-Regel in der Query-API:**
375	| Query-Type | Backend |
376	|---|---|
377	| `get_entity`, `get_fact`, `propose_fact`, Provenance-Joins, Time-Travel | Postgres |
378	| Multi-Hop-Pattern (`MATCH (a)-[*..3]->(b)`), Shortest-Path, GDS-Algos | Neo4j |
379	| Hybrid Search (`search_memory`) | Postgres pgvector ∩ Neo4j edges → Postgres rerank |
380	
381	**Skalierbarkeits-Argument (Pitch-Antwort):** *"Postgres skaliert transaktional zu Milliarden Rows — Episode-Store, Audit-Log, Provenance bleiben hier. Neo4j skaliert Graph-Traversal zu 100M+ Edges — Multi-Hop-Queries und Pattern-Matching gehen dort. Jeder Layer skaliert in seinem Domain."*
382	
383	**Failure-Mode:** Sync-Crash = Neo4j-Projection wird stale, **nichts korrumpiert**. Re-Sync via Replay aus Postgres möglich. Kein Dual-Write-Risiko weil Postgres die einzige Schreibseite ist.
384	
385	**Hard-Cap:** Samstag 14:00. Wenn Sync-Worker bis dahin nicht stabil → Neo4j wird im Pitch als "Day 2" gestreamt, Demo läuft auf Postgres-only.
386	
387	### Change Streams
388	
389	Apps können auf Events subscriben:
390	
391	```
392	SUBSCRIBE: entity_status_change where type=Deal
393	SUBSCRIBE: new_fact where category=champion_activity
394	```
395	
396	Downstream-Apps reagieren reactive, ohne zu pollen.
397	
398	### Was der Layer NICHT tut
399	
400	- Keine Business-Logik (Was ein „guter Deal" ist, ist App-Wissen)
401	- Keine Notifications
402	- Keine Action-Generation
403	- Keine domain-spezifischen Begriffe (keine „Opportunities", „Champions", „Stages" als eingebaute Konzepte)
404	- **Kein Background-Worker für Re-Derivation.** `source_hash` + `extractor_version` werden gespeichert, Re-Derivation läuft on-demand bzw. bei Re-Ingest desselben Records. Kein Cron.
405	
406	Der Layer kennt nur Entitäten, Beziehungen, Zeit, Attribution.
407	
408	> **Note (2026-04-25):** frühere Cuts auf "VFS read-only" und "keine Ambiguity-Inbox-UI" wurden zurückgenommen. Vollständige Anforderungs-Abdeckung in [Full Requirements Coverage](#full-requirements-coverage-option-a) unten.
409	
410	---
411	
412	## Layer 2: Revenue Intelligence App
413	
414	Läuft als normaler Client gegen die Query-API des Core-Layers. Importiert nichts Internes.
415	
416	### Business-Logik (hier, nicht im Core)
417	
418	Alle Revenue-Semantik ist App-Code:
419	
420	- Pattern-Definitions (Was ist ein At-Risk-Deal? Was ist Churn-Signal?)
421	- Priorisierungs-Heuristiken
422	- Action-Templates und Draft-Generation
423	- User-Preference-Learning
424	
425	### Kern-Features für Demo
426	
427	**Morning Action Feed:**
428	Cron-Job läuft vor Demo-Zeit. Queried den Core-Layer nach Patterns. Generiert priorisierte Action-Liste. Jede Action hat Finding, Evidence, Hypothesis, Recommended Action, Prepared Artifact.
429	
430	**Deal Evidence View:**
431	Single-Deal-Drilldown. Zeigt alle Facts die den Deal-Status begründen, mit Attribution zum Core-Layer. Demonstriert dass die App nur zusammenträgt was der Layer liefert.
432	
433	**Multi-Role-Views:**
434	Drei UI-Sichten (Sales, CS, Leadership) die alle auf derselben Core-Layer-Data operieren. Beweis der Horizontalität.
435	
436	### Draft-Generation
437	
438	LLM-basiert mit Context vom Core-Layer. Bei Draft-Generation wird explizit Attribution mitgegeben:
439	
440	- Welche Facts aus Layer wurden genutzt
441	- Welche Stakeholder-Context
442	- Welche historischen Communications als Style-Template
443	
444	---
445	
446	## Layer 3: Second App (nur für Demo)
447	
448	Minimale zweite App, die am Pitch-Ende live gestartet wird. Zeigt dass der Core-Layer wirklich horizontal ist.
449	
450	Vorschlag: Eine simple HR-Onboarding-View oder eine Finance-Briefing-View. Zeigt dieselben Core-Layer-Daten durch eine komplett andere Brille.
451	
452	Minimal in UI, minimal in Logic. Zweck: beweisen dass neue Apps in Stunden schreibbar sind wenn Core-Layer existiert.
453	
454	Nicht vorher hyped — Demo-Moment kommt als Überraschung am Ende.
455	
456	---
457	
458	## Tech-Stack-Empfehlungen (vom Team)
459	
460	> **Note (2026-04-24):** diese Team-Empfehlungen wurden bereits teilweise durch gemeinsame Entscheidungen aktualisiert — siehe [`docs/stack.md`](stack.md) für den finalen Stack (Vite+React, Supabase als Single-DB statt Neo4j+Qdrant, Gemini als LLM).
461	
462	**Core Layer:**
463	
464	- Runtime: Node.js oder Python (was das Team schneller kann)
465	- Graph-DB: Neo4j (hat die beste Node.js/Python-Integration) oder Postgres mit recursive CTEs als Fallback
466	- Vector-Store für Entity-Resolution: Qdrant oder pgvector (wenn Postgres eh da ist)
467	- API-Layer: GraphQL oder REST — was schneller geht, beides ist akzeptabel
468	
469	**Revenue App:**
470	
471	- Frontend: Next.js mit Lovable für UI-Speed
472	- Backend: API-Client gegen Core-Layer
473	- LLM: Claude via Anthropic API (längerer Context besser für Draft-Generation)
474	
475	**Ingestion:**
476	
477	- Mock-Connectors die aus JSON-Files ingestieren statt echte APIs abzugreifen
478	- Spart Setup-Zeit, für Demo irrelevant ob Mock oder Real
479	
480	**Deployment:**
481	
482	- Vercel für Frontend
483	- Supabase oder Railway für Backend + DB
484	
485	---
486	
487	## Build-Priorisierung für 48h
488	
489	> **Note (2026-04-25, Option A — voll erfüllen):** Verteilung neu gewichtet. Alle 10 Anforderungs-Sektionen werden gecovered. Revenue App gekürzt zugunsten Core-Vollständigkeit + Human-UI.
490	
491	**45% Zeit auf Core Layer (Vollständig):**
492	
493	1. Hand-rolled Cascade-Resolver für Person + Company + Product (Pioneer-Tier 3.5)
494	2. VFS mit Read **+ Write/Delete** (Write/Delete intern via `propose_fact` / `mark_fact_invalid`)
495	3. Query API (REST + 5 MCP-Tools + `get_fact_provenance`)
496	4. Graph Construction für die 9 Edge-Types, konfigurierbar via YAML-Ontologien
497	5. Source Attribution durchgängig (system/path/record-id/timestamp/method + confidence)
498	6. Bi-temporal Schema (tstzrange + EXCLUDE + Supersede-Trigger), eine "as-of"-Query
499	7. Auto-Resolution-Rules (Recency / Authority / Confidence / Cross-Confirmation)
500	8. Resolution-Decision Traceability (`resolution_signals: jsonb`)
501	9. PDF-Adapter mit Text + 1 strukturiertem Schema (Invoice oder Resume)
502	10. Diff in Change-Events (über `superseded_by` Pointer)
503	11. Document/Communication als first-class Entity-Types
504	12. Two-Tier Embeddings (Name-default + Inference-rich für hot entities)
505	13. Hybrid Search (`search_memory` als Semantic ∩ Structural → Rerank)
506	
507	**5% Zeit auf Neo4j-Projection (NEU, Tag 1 hard-cap Samstag 14:00):**
508	
509	1. Neo4j Aura Free-Tier setup
510	2. `server/sync/neo4j_projection.py` — Supabase-Realtime-Listener → idempotent MERGE-Cypher
511	3. Cypher-Query-Endpoint in FastAPI für Demo-Queries
512	4. Eine Cypher-Wow-Demo-Query getestet (3-Hop-Pattern)
513	
514	**Hard-Cap-Regel:** Wenn Samstag 14:00 nicht steht → Postgres-only weiter, Neo4j wird "Day 2" im Pitch.
515	
516	**20% Zeit auf Human-Facing UI (NEU, Pflicht):**
517	
518	1. **VFS-Browser** mit Detail-Views pro Node (klickbar, full-detail)
519	2. **Pending-Review-Queue** für Ambiguity-Pairs + Conflicts (Pick-One / Merge / Reject Buttons)
520	3. **Validate-UI:** "Looks correct" / "Flag as wrong" Buttons pro Fact-Card
521	4. **Edit-UI:** Manuelle Korrekturen mit Edit-History (schreibt SourceRecord)
522	5. **Volltext + Graph-Search**
523	
524	**15% Zeit auf Revenue App (gekürzt):**
525	
526	1. Sales Action Feed mit **3** prebuilt Patterns (statt 5-7)
527	2. Deal Evidence View für **1** Demo-Deal
528	3. Draft-Generation für **2** Action-Types
529	
530	**Gestrichen:** CS-View, Leadership-View, mehrere Patterns. Eine glasklare Sales-Story reicht.
531	
532	**10% Zeit auf Eval-Harness + Demo-Killer-Features (Tag 1!):**
533	
534	1. **Eval-Harness** mit 6-8 reproduzierbaren Demo-Questions (`question → expected facts → cited sources`)
535	2. **Time-Travel-Slider** in der Entity-Detail-View — UI-Slider pin't `recorded_at`, Entity-Card-State updated live. *Single feature outweighs three of your other planned demos.*
536	3. **Trust-Score auf jeder Entity-Card:** `trust = avg(confidence) × source_diversity × recency_decay`. Drei Zeilen SQL, riesige perceived sophistication. Erfüllt direkt Per-Source Trust-Weighting (Req 6.2).
537	4. **Streaming Ingestion Log** via Supabase Realtime (SourceRecord → Facts → Resolutions live tail)
538	5. **GDPR Source-Delete-Cascade** (~30 Zeilen, Killer für Enterprise-Judges)
539	6. **Pioneer-vs-Gemini Comparison-View** — side-by-side Tabelle für 10 Beispiele. Belegt den Pioneer-Side-Prize claim glaubhaft.
540	7. **Cypher-Wow-Query auf Neo4j-Projection** — z.B. *"Find all Persons connected to Acme within 3 hops, group by relationship type"* oder *"Shortest path between Alice and any Customer"*. Pitch-Story: "Postgres skaliert transaktional, Neo4j skaliert Multi-Hop — jeder Layer in seinem Domain."
541	
542	**5% Zeit auf Second App + Pitch-Integration:**
543	Minimal. HR-View auf denselben Core-Daten. Demo-Transitions testen.
544	
545	---
546	
547	## Demo-Flow (Technical View)
548	
549	Der Demo-Moment muss beide Layer zeigen.
550	
551	**Akt 1: Layer Live-Demo (90 Sekunden)**
552	Browser-Tab mit Core-Layer-UI (minimal debug-View). Live-Ingestion von 2-3 Quellen sichtbar. Entity-Resolution-Visualisierung („Max in Source A wurde resolved zu Entity X"). Eine Query-API-Demonstration im Browser-DevTools oder simpler Admin-UI.
553	
554	**Akt 2: Revenue App (120 Sekunden)**
555	Neuer Browser-Tab. Revenue-App zeigt Morning-Feed. Jury-Member interagiert. Draft wird generiert. Evidence-Chain wird gezeigt. Alle Daten kommen beweisbar aus Query-API-Calls (Network-Tab könnte gezeigt werden).
556	
557	**Akt 3: Second App (45 Sekunden)**
558	Dritter Browser-Tab. Zweite App läuft, zeigt dieselben Daten in anderer Brille. Betonung: Core-Layer unverändert.
559	
560	---
561	
562	## Risiken und Mitigationen
563	
564	**Risiko: Entity Resolution funktioniert live schlecht.**
565	Mitigation: Pre-resolved Demo-Daten mit explizit getesteten Fällen. Live-Resolution nur für 1-2 hochkontrollierte Beispiele.
566	
567	**Risiko: Core-Layer sieht aus wie Database-Wrapper.**
568	Mitigation: Explizit VFS-Path-Struktur und Graph-Visualisierung in UI zeigen. Nicht nur „wir haben eine DB mit API" sondern „wir haben einen structured state mit navigierbarer Ontologie."
569	
570	**Risiko: Revenue-App wirkt wie Main-Product und Layer als Nebenwerk.**
571	Mitigation: In Demo-Choreografie den Layer als Hero-Element framen, Revenue-App als Proof. Pitch-Sprache: „Wir haben die Infrastruktur gebaut. Die Revenue-App ist der erste Beweis."
572	
573	**Risiko: Zu viele Features, keins funktioniert richtig.**
574	Mitigation: Scope-Brutalität. Was nicht auf der obigen Prioritäts-Liste steht, wird verworfen. Lieber 4 Features die glänzen als 8 die stottern.
575	
576	**Risiko: Wir bauen Splink/Cognee/Graphiti von Grund auf nach.**
577	Mitigation: Splink in Hour 1 pip-install + minimal-pipeline. Cognee-Schema vor erstem Migration-File lesen. Graphiti-Extraction-Prompts kopieren + auf Gemini umschreiben. Wer "ich schreib das schnell selbst" sagt, wird overruled.
578	
579	**Risiko: Option-A-Scope (alle 10 Requirements) frisst Demo-Polish.**
580	Mitigation: Revenue App ist auf 3 Patterns / 1 Deal-View / 2 Action-Types reduziert. Eval-Harness auf 6-8 Questions. Second App auf nackte HR-View. Wenn am Sonntag 8:00 noch was wackelt: Revenue-Patterns weiter cutten, niemals Core- oder Human-UI-Requirements.
581	
582	**Risiko: Human-UI wird Klick-Demo statt funktional.**
583	Mitigation: Validate/Pending-Review-Queue/Edit ALLE schreiben echte SourceRecords — keine Fake-Buttons. Eval-Harness testet, dass Edit→Re-Query den geänderten Fact zurückgibt.
584	
585	---
586	
587	## Full Requirements Coverage (Option A)
588	
589	Verbindliche Coverage der 10 Anforderungs-Sektionen aus dem externen Requirements-Brief. Status: **alles must-build**, nichts mehr "nice-to-have".
590	
591	### 1. Data Ingestion
592	- **1.1** Multi-Format: JSON, JSONL, CSV, **PDF** (text via `pdfplumber`, structured fields via Gemini + `instructor` für ≥1 Doc-Type). Doclinq als optionaler Adapter.
593	- **1.2** Plug-in-Connector-Architektur. Interface dokumentiert in `docs/connector-template.md`. Neuer Connector in <30 Min.
594	- **1.3** PDF-Strukturextraktion: ein Pydantic-Schema pro Doc-Type (Invoice oder Resume für Demo).
595	- **1.4** Idempotent: `content_hash` + deterministische IDs. Re-Ingest = Update, kein Duplikat.
596	
597	### 2. Virtual File System
598	- **2.1** Hierarchische Pfade `/companies/<id>/deals/<id>/...`
599	- **2.2** Jeder Node: path, type, content, metadata, source_reference, timestamps.
600	- **2.3** Operations: List, Read, **Write, Delete, Glob-Search**. Write/Delete intern via `propose_fact` / `mark_fact_invalid` — Markdown bleibt deterministische Projection, aber API erfüllt Anforderung.
601	- **2.4** Browser-UI als Product-Surface, jeder Node klickbar mit Full-Detail-View.
602	
603	### 3. Entity Resolution
604	- **3.1** Cross-Source via Splink + pgvector-Blocking.
605	- **3.2** Entity-Types: `Person`, `Company`, `Product`, **`Document`, `Communication`** (alle first-class).
606	- **3.3** Signale: ID-Match → Structured-IDs → Embedding-Similarity → Context-Heuristics.
607	- **3.4** Resolution-Decisions traceable: Splink-Match-Weights pro Comparison-Column werden in `resolution_signals: jsonb` gespeichert. Decision-View zeigt "warum hat das System gemerged".
608	- **3.5** Pending-Review-Queue (UI!) statt Auto-Merge bei Score 0.5–0.95.
609	
610	### 4. Graph Construction
611	- **4.1** Resolved Entities = Nodes, Beziehungen = typisierte Edges.
612	- **4.2** Edge-Types: `works_at`, `manages`, `authored`, `references`, `mentions`, `owns`, `assigned_to`, `participant_in`, `related_to`. + use-case-spezifische via Config.
613	- **4.3** Edge-Metadata: source, confidence, valid_from/to.
614	- **4.4** Bi-direktional: Indexes auf `(subject_id, predicate)` UND `(object_id, predicate)`.
615	- **4.5** Inkrementell beim Ingest aufgebaut.
616	
617	### 5. Source Attribution
618	- **5.1** Jeder Fact zwingend attribuiert. DB-Constraint: `derived_from` NOT NULL und non-empty.
619	- **5.2** Source-Reference enthält: `system`, `path/endpoint`, `record_id`, `timestamp`, **`method`** (z.B. "regex_extract", "llm_gemini_2.0", "human_input").
620	- **5.3** Confidence-Score Pflicht bei LLM/Inferenz-Extraktion (DB-Constraint: `method LIKE 'llm_%'` → `confidence IS NOT NULL`).
621	- **5.4** Provenance über Query-API: dediziertes MCP-Tool `get_fact_provenance(fact_id)` + REST-Endpoint.
622	- **5.5** Rückverfolgbar bis Original-SourceRecord (über `derived_from` → SourceRecord-Row mit `source_uri`).
623	
624	### 6. Conflict Detection & Resolution
625	- **6.1** Auto-Detection bei (subject, predicate)-Kollision mit unterschiedlichem `object`. EXCLUDE-Constraint triggert; conflicting Facts bekommen `status: disputed`.
626	- **6.2** Auto-Resolution-Rules als Cascade:
627	  1. **Recency:** neuere `valid_from` schlägt ältere bei gleicher Source-Authority
628	  2. **Authority:** `source_trust_score` aus Per-Source-Trust-Weighting-Tabelle
629	  3. **Confidence:** höhere Confidence gewinnt
630	  4. **Cross-Confirmation:** Fact mit ≥2 unabhängigen `derived_from` schlägt Single-Source
631	- **6.3** Pending-Review-Queue mit UI (Liste, Pick-One, Merge, Reject).
632	- **6.4** Manual Resolutions als Audit-Trail in SourceRecord(`type=human_resolution`).
633	
634	### 7. Query API
635	- **7.1** Path-based: `GET /nodes/{path}` + Glob.
636	- **7.2** Graph-Traversal: `POST /query/traverse` mit Cypher-ähnlichem JSON-DSL.
637	- **7.3** Jede Response: `{value, confidence, evidence: [{source, ...}]}`.
638	- **7.4** REST + JSON. MCP-Tools auf gleiche Funktionen mappable.
639	
640	### 8. Human-Facing UI (Pflicht-Sektion, vorher gestrichen, jetzt in)
641	- **8.1** Inspect: VFS-Browser mit Detail-Views.
642	- **8.2** Validate: "Looks correct" / "Flag as wrong" Buttons pro Fact-Card → schreiben Validation-SourceRecord.
643	- **8.3** Edit: Manuelle Korrekturen mit Edit-History (jede Edit = neuer SourceRecord).
644	- **8.4** Conflict-Review-Queue (= 6.3).
645	- **8.5** Volltext + Graph-Search.
646	
647	### 9. Generalization (Qontext-kritisch)
648	- **9.1** Kein Code hartcoded auf EnterpriseBench. Datenpfade in Config.
649	- **9.2** Connector-Interface dokumentiert (<30 Min für neuen Connector). Skeleton + Beispiel im Repo.
650	- **9.3** Entity-/Edge-Types konfigurierbar via `entity_type_config` / `edge_type_config` Tabellen, init via JSON-Seed.
651	- **9.4** Keine Use-Case-Semantik im Core. Review-Kriterium für jeden PR.
652	
653	### 10. Update Propagation
654	- **10.1** Source-Changes: Polling (für Mock-Files via FS-Watch) und Webhook-Endpoint vorbereitet.
655	- **10.2** Change-Events subscribebar via Supabase Realtime auf `facts` und `entities`.
656	- **10.3** Diff-Information in Events: über `superseded_by`-Pointer kann Pre-Fact mitgeladen werden; API-Layer komponiert `{old, new}` payload.
657	
658	### Engineer-Entscheidungen
659	- **Mit Rückfrage:** Tech-Stack, Scope-Cuts, Default-Sets für Entity-/Edge-Types.
660	- **Selbst:** Implementation-Details, Code-Struktur, Library-Choices, Testing-Approach.
661	
662	### Nicht mehr verhandelbar
663	Kein "we can ship this without UI" mehr. Kein "ambiguity inbox is data-only" mehr. Kein "VFS read-only" mehr. Diese Cuts wurden 2026-04-25 zurückgenommen.
664	
665	---
666	
667	## Adopted OSS — nicht selbst nachbauen
668	
669	Aus der Cowork-Research (2026-04-25). Wer "ich schreib das schnell selbst" sagt, wird overruled. **Wer "ich integriere noch schnell Cognee/Splink" sagt, wird auch overruled.**
670	
671	**Pip install:**
672	- **[instructor](https://github.com/instructor-ai/instructor)** — strukturierte Gemini-Outputs für Fact-Extraction. **Hard must.** Rettet vor JSON-Parsing-Hell.
673	- **rapidfuzz** — String-Distance-Fallback in C++.
674	- **pdfplumber** — PDF-Text-Extraktion.
675	- **pgvector + HNSW** — schon im Stack.
676	
677	**Steal patterns, do NOT import:**
678	- **[Cognee](https://github.com/topoteretes/cognee)** — DataPoints-Idee (typed Pydantic + Postgres-Mirror) klauen. Nicht runtime-integrieren — Postgres-only-Retrofit kostet 6h.
679	- **[Graphiti](https://github.com/getzep/graphiti)** (Zep) — Edge-Property-Pattern + Bi-temporal-Invalidation + Extraction-Prompts ansehen. **Nicht installieren**, auch nicht jetzt wo wir Neo4j haben. Begründung: End-to-End-Pipeline (würde 60% unserer Architektur ersetzen), Bi-temporal-Konflikt (Postgres EXCLUDE vs Graphiti App-Logic), Pioneer-Story kaputt, 48h-Lernkurve. Patterns klauen, Runtime nicht. **Hard rule:** wer am Samstag "lass mal Graphiti einbauen" sagt → 60min Begründungs-Pflicht oder overruled.
680	- **[LightRAG](https://github.com/HKUDS/LightRAG)** — Dual-Level-Retrieval-Pattern in `search_memory` MCP-Tool replizieren (entity-mention + topical phase). Lib nicht ziehen.
681	
682	**Don't even click the docs (Traps):**
683	- **[Splink](https://github.com/moj-analytical-services/splink)** — Will Labeled-Pairs + EM-Training. 6-10h Time-to-Value, hand-rolled Cascade ist 4h. **Zurückgenommen 2026-04-25.**
684	- **Senzing** — closed-source, Demo-opaque, Lizenz-Trap.
685	- **Zingg** — Spark-Setup eats 3h.
686	- **OpenLineage/Marquez** — falsche Abstraktionsebene (Dataset-level statt Fact-level).
687	- **LangChain Graph** — Toy-Abstraktion.
688	- **GraphRAG** (Microsoft) — Leiden-Communities + per-Community-LLM-Summaries. $500-650 Indexing-Cost auf Real-Benchmarks. Demo-Slide-Candy.
689	- **temporal_tables** — C-Extension, Supabase ships sie nicht.
690	- **HippoRAG / PPR** — needs in-memory graph. Faken via Two-Hop-SQL falls überhaupt.
691	
692	## Partner-Tech-Allokation
693	
694	Vollständige Spec in [`partner-tech.md`](partner-tech.md). Kurzform:
695	
696	| Partner | Wofür | Time-Box |
697	|---|---|---|
698	| **Pioneer (Fastino)** | GLiNER2-finetune für E+R-Extraction am Hot-Path (Cascade Tier 3.5). Synthetic Data aus EnterpriseBench via Gemini. | **6h hard cap.** Fallback = Gemini Flash. |
699	| **Google DeepMind (Gemini)** | Embeddings (`text-embedding-004`), Ambiguity-Resolution (`gemini-2.0-flash`), Draft-Generation + multimodal PDF (`gemini-2.5-pro`), Eval-Harness | drüber verteilt |
700	| **Entire** | Dev-Tool: Provenance + Checkpoints fürs Coding-Workflow. **Nicht im Produkt verbaut.** | Day-1 CLI-Setup, ~30min |
701	| **Aikido** | Repo-Security-Scan, Side-Prize 1000€ | Day-1 Connect, ~30min |
702	
703	---
704	
705	## Was Engineers wissen müssen bevor sie Code schreiben
706	
707	1. Core-Layer-Code darf keine Revenue-Terminologie enthalten. Review-Kriterium.
708	2. Alle Revenue-App-Daten kommen über Query-API, nie direct DB-Access. Review-Kriterium.
709	3. Attribution muss in jedem API-Response enthalten sein. Nicht optional.
710	4. Temporal-State kann im MVP simpler sein (nur `valid_from`/`valid_to`), aber darf nicht komplett fehlen.
711	5. Wenn ihr vor einer Design-Entscheidung steht die „für Revenue wäre es so einfacher" sagt: Die horizontale Variante gewinnt.
712	
713	---
714	
715	## Offene technische Entscheidungen — RESOLVED (2026-04-25)
716	
717	1. ~~Graph-DB vs Postgres~~ → **Postgres mit reified Facts in einer Tabelle** (siehe Temporal-State-Schema oben). Keine Neo4j.
718	2. ~~GraphQL vs REST~~ → **REST + MCP-Server** (5 Tools). Siehe [`docs/mcp-tools.md`](mcp-tools.md).
719	3. ~~LLM-Provider~~ → **Gemini Flash**, ausschließlich für Ambiguity-Resolution (post-Splink) und Draft-Generation. **Nicht Hot-Path-Matcher.**
720	4. ~~Mock vs Real Connectors~~ → **Mock-from-JSON, max 2 Sources** (Email + CRM).
